runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) on EITC and labor supply
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) on welfare reform and single mothers' employment
Saez (2010) on behavioral responses to tax changes
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers show relatively elastic labor supply responses compared to married women or men, likely due to discrete work/non-work choices and fixed costs of employment. Medium-run elasticity around 0.6 reflects empirical findings from tax policy and welfare reform studies, with substantial uncertainty (0.2 to 1.2 range) due to variation in methodology, time horizons, and policy contexts.

Medium-run elasticity around 0.6 reflects empirical findings from tax policy and welfare reform studies, with substantial uncertainty (0.2 to 1.2 range) due to variation in methodology, time horizons, and policy contexts."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers with respect to the net-of-tax wage
citations: Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) labor supply elasticity literature
Eissa & Liebman (1996) EITC employment effects
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) labor force participation responses to tax/transfer changes
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers face high work incentives and are a primary policy-responsive population. Empirical evidence from EITC expansions and welfare reforms suggests elasticities in the 0.2–0.6 range for this group. Medium-run estimates tend to be larger than very short-run due to adjustment lags but smaller than long-run as human capital constraints bind. I center on 0.35 with substantial uncertainty reflecting heterogeneity in responses and methodological variation across studies.

Empirical evidence from EITC expansions and welfare reforms suggests elasticities in the 0.2\u20130.6 range for this group. Medium-run estimates tend to be larger than very short-run due to adjustment lags but smaller than long-run as human capital constraints bind. I center on 0.35 with substantial uncertainty reflecting heterogeneity in responses and methodological variation across studies."}

Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) labor supply elasticities review

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers face substantial fixed costs of work, making them responsive to wage incentives. Medium-run elasticity likely exceeds long-run estimates (which cluster around 0.3) but less than short-run responses. EITC expansions show participation responses in the 0.3-0.6 range. Uncertainty reflects heterogeneity in labor market constraints and competing income/substitution effects, with some upward bias toward higher estimates given policy experiment results.

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers face relatively high labor supply elasticity to wages due to childcare constraints and lower reservation wages. Medium-run estimates from EITC reforms and similar quasi-experiments typically find participation elasticities in the 0.3-1.0 range. Higher uncertainty at upper tail reflects heterogeneity across margin (extensive vs. intensive) and policy design.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers with respect to the net-of-tax wage: percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage
citations: Eissa and Liebman (1996) on EITC and labor supply responses
Meyer and Rosenbaum studies on labor supply elasticities for low-income mothers
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) labor supply survey
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers typically exhibit moderate positive wage elasticity of labor supply, higher than secondary earners but often lower than conventional estimates for other groups due to childcare constraints and fixed costs of work. Medium-run elasticity (1-3 years) should reflect some behavioral adjustment. Wage elasticities for this population in earned income tax credit research and labor supply studies cluster in the 0.2-0.4 range, with substantial uncertainty reflecting variation in methodology and context. Conservative interval reflects both the evidence of positive response and material remaining uncertainty.

Conservative interval reflects both the evidence of positive response and material remaining uncertainty."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) on tax policy and single mothers
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) on welfare reform and work
Blundell & Macurdy (1999) on labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are generally regarded as having relatively high labor supply elasticities compared to married women and men, owing to substantial non-employment and flexibility in hours. Empirical work on EITC expansions and welfare reform suggests elasticities in the 0.2-0.5 range for employment participation decisions. Medium-run estimates are larger than long-run behavioral responses but smaller than extreme short-run adjustments. The central estimate of 0.35 reflects modest but meaningful responsiveness to wage incentives, with meaningful uncertainty spanning from quite inelastic to moderately elastic outcomes.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States", "point_estimate": 0.35, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.1, "p25": 0.22, "p50": 0.35, "p75": 0.5, "p95": 0.75}, "citations": ["Eissa & Liebman (1996) on tax policy and single mothers", "Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) on welfare reform and work", "Blundell & Macurdy (1999) on labor supply elasticities"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are generally regarded as having relatively high labor supply elasticities compared to married women and men, owing to substantial non-employment and flexibility in hours. Empirical work on EITC expansions and welfare reform suggests elasticities in the 0.2-0.5 range for employment participation decisions. Medium-run estimates are larger than long-run behavioral responses but smaller than extreme short-run adjustments. The central estimate of 0.35 reflects modest but meaningful responsiveness to wage incentives, with meaningful uncertainty spanning from quite inelastic to moderately elastic outcomes."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) on tax policy and labor supply of single mothers
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) on welfare reform and employment
Keane & Moffitt (1998) on life-cycle labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers exhibit relatively elastic labor supply responses to wage changes compared to other groups due to childcare constraints and work-family tradeoffs. Medium-run estimates typically range from 0.3 to 0.7, with substantial uncertainty around the point estimate. The distribution reflects empirical variation across tax experiments, welfare reforms, and structural models. Higher elasticities for this group compared to married women are supported by sensitivity to marginal incentives.

Medium-run estimates typically range from 0.3 to 0.7, with substantial uncertainty around the point estimate. The distribution reflects empirical variation across tax experiments, welfare reforms, and structural models. Higher elasticities for this group compared to married women are supported by sensitivity to marginal incentives."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers with respect to the net-of-tax wage
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) on EITC expansions and labor supply responses
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) on single mother labor supply elasticities
Saez (2010) on behavioral responses to tax changes
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers face stronger labor supply incentives than the general population due to financial necessity, potential welfare interactions, and childcare constraints. Literature on EITC expansions and welfare reform suggests elasticities in the 0.5–0.8 range for participation decisions in the medium run. The distribution reflects uncertainty about mechanism (wages vs. subsidies), heterogeneous responses, and whether elasticity differs between entry and exit margins. Values above 1.0 are possible but less likely than moderate elasticities.

Literature on EITC expansions and welfare reform suggests elasticities in the 0.5\u20130.8 range for participation decisions in the medium run. The distribution reflects uncertainty about mechanism (wages vs. subsidies), heterogeneous responses, and whether elasticity differs between entry and exit margins. Values above 1.0 are possible but less likely than moderate elasticities."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers exhibit substantial but not fully elastic labor supply responses to wage changes. Medium-run elasticity reflects adjustment to childcare constraints and reduced extensive margin effects. Empirical literature on EITC and welfare reform suggests elasticities in the 0.3-0.6 range for this population, with considerable heterogeneity. Wider tail distribution reflects uncertainty about how wage responses vary across policy contexts and demographic subgroups.
